bit cold where I'm at, to be honest.

So GA4 generally, again, the number of sessions seem to be a little...
little lower than expected.

But generally speaking, the data is a little iffy.
So the approach we're taking here is as long as there's nothing super egregious, we're just assuming that performance is going to go well and that we're hitting the same levels.
So currently, as I said, the overall website traffic also declined the same proportion as what you see in this chart, which is just the GrowthX URLs.
So we feel there's some latent GSC issues just coming from the past few weeks.
And then, you know, with that caveat, again, according to GSC, we have 138 evaluation related keywords in the top 10.
Sorry, my.
And then going to the individual cohort performance, there has been a lot of position volatility over the past few weeks.
Despite that, we are ranking in the top 10 for almost every pillar.
Observability is the only one that's lagging a little bit.
This kind of makes sense because a lot of our content is more geared towards evaluation and reliability so far.
Guardrails is a relatively new sort of pillar where we've been expanding content.
So we've published a lot of high-level, you could say, guide sort of content, like how to establish Guardrails, et cetera.
And that content seems to be doing pretty well.
We're in the top 10 already, so pretty promising performance there.
And then generally speaking, with regards to refreshes, since our content is already ranking pretty high because these average ranks are quite high as it is, a lot of the refreshes are...
More about maintaining performance.
So we're not expecting to generate massive click boosts from them.
So I just want to make that pretty clear.

The majority of the click gains we expect to come from the model app, the competitor pages, and the new blogs that we're creating, which it's a reduced volume, but still we do expect new click gains to come from there.
Just a top 10 ranking update.
So Galileo is at 329 and Arise is at 906.

One reason, again, we're not closing the gap as quickly as possible is because Arise ranks a lot for general LLM review sort of content, whereas we've been prioritizing a bit deeper funnel content in terms of all of our pillars.
And then LLM visibility.
So over the past seven days, generally, we see to a pull closer to brain trust in terms of overall visibility.
And obviously up next is Arise and Langsmith and Langfuse.
And then, generally speaking, some of the numbers suggest that Galileo's presence is quite low.
But again, as I mentioned last week, most of this is because a lot of the enterprise searches are not highlighting either Galileo or Galileo's competitors.
They're highlighting other enterprise solutions.
So that's why this 18% number looks a bit alarming at first.
But if you look at this next sort of 6% to 9% number in the bottom right, it kind of makes more sense.
Digging deeper into the prompts, prompt clusters themselves, we're seeing some very positive trends.
So for AI agent evaluation prompts, we're trailing just LangSmith.
We're ahead of LangFuse, which is in third position.
And then for LLM evals, we're trailing just LangSmith and LangFuse.
We're ahead of Arise, BrainTrust, et cetera.
In terms of AI agent reliability, safety risk management, and GenAI guardrails, we are showing up far more often.
And then the competition.
And then we're generally in the top three for almost every prompt cluster.
We're lagging only in observability for LLM and agents.
And as I mentioned, this is because we just generally have lesser content in this particular pillar.
So that's the performance overview.
Any questions or anything I can clarify over here?
No, that all makes sense.
I know we've been putting out some more observability-focused blogs in the last few weeks.
So that should help.
And I know we have the pillar page coming soon, which should also help.

So we were making gains there.
And I think as long as we keep fitting in some net new blogs around the topic in the future pipeline, that will help with that.
As far as the Google data is concerned.
and

If there's any other questions or clarifications we can answer for you.

Suggest that we do the first couple as blogs.

Or if we want to do the same sort of like pillar page style format and then turn blogs into that.
So still debating there and would love any feedback.
Yeah, generally, I would certainly recommend having standalone pages because I think that just addresses every need.
I would certainly index them, you know.
But yeah, we can certainly start off with blogs.
There is no reason to stop with that.
But eventually, the ideal scenario is we should have landing pages for all of these.
Because they tend to do quite well.

So it can't hurt.
